subject: french
explainer: "Je m'appelle" literally means "I call myself". Use it to introduce yourself.

subject: french
prompt: How do you say "I am 12 years old" in French?
explainer: In French you HAVE your age — "j'ai" ("I have") not "je suis" ("I am").

subject: french
explainer: Douze = 12. Useful sequence: dix, onze, douze, treize, quatorze, quinze.

subject: french
explainer: Frère = brother. Sister is sœur. Petit frère = little brother.

subject: french
explainer: Ma sœur = my sister. "Ma" is feminine "my"; brother would use "mon".

subject: french
explainer: Mai = May. Easy to remember — almost the same word.

subject: french
prompt: What is the French word for "September"?
explainer: Septembre. Most months are similar to English: janvier, février, mars, avril, mai…

subject: french
prompt: How do you write "30" in French?
explainer: Trente = 30. Don't confuse with treize (13).

subject: french
explainer: Vingt = 20, deux = 2, so vingt-deux = 22.

subject: french
prompt: What does "je suis de Paris" mean?
explainer: "Je suis de" = "I am from". Use it for the place you come from.

subject: french
explainer: Soixante (60) + dix (10) = 70. French builds 70 as "sixty-ten".

subject: french
prompt: What does "j'ai les cheveux blonds" mean?
explainer: Cheveux = hair (always plural). Blonds = blonde.

subject: french
prompt: How do you say "I have green eyes" in French?
explainer: Yeux = eyes. Verts = green (plural to match les yeux).

subject: french
prompt: What does "courts" mean when describing hair?
explainer: Courts = short (cheveux courts). Long is "longs".

subject: french
explainer: Une barbe = a beard. Une moustache = a moustache.

subject: french
prompt: What does "je porte des lunettes" mean?
explainer: Porter = to wear. Lunettes = glasses (always plural in French).

subject: french
prompt: "J'ai les yeux marron." What colour are the eyes?
explainer: Marron = brown. Strange feature: "marron" doesn't change for plural — always "yeux marron".

subject: french
prompt: What does "je vis dans une grande maison" mean?
options: I live in a big house
explainer: Vivre = to live. "Je vis" = "I live". Grande = big (feminine, to match maison).
audio: Je vis dans une grande maison

subject: french
explainer: Un appartement = a flat. Une maison = a house.

subject: french
explainer: La côte = the coast. Dans le centre = in the centre. Dans la banlieue = on the outskirts.

subject: french
explainer: Grand-mère = grandmother. Grand-père = grandfather.

subject: french
prompt: How do you say "I get on well with my brother"?
options: Je m'entends bien avec mon frère
Je vais bien avec mon frère
Je suis bien avec mon frère
explainer: Je m'entends bien avec… = I get on well with… "Je m'entends mal avec…" = I don't get on well with…
audio: Je m'entends bien avec mon frère

subject: french
explainer: Mon oncle = my uncle. Ma tante = my aunty.

subject: french
prompt: Choose the correct form: "Ma sœur est ___" (tall).
explainer: Sœur is feminine singular, so the adjective takes -e: "grande". For a brother (masculine) it would be "grand".

subject: french
prompt: Choose the correct form: "Mon père est ___" (fun).
explainer: Père is masculine singular, so the adjective stays in its base form: "marrant".

subject: french
prompt: How do you say "shy" (masculine) in French?
explainer: Timide = shy. Useful: "timide" doesn't change for masculine vs feminine — same form.

subject: french
prompt: What do "très / assez / un peu" mean?
explainer: These are intensifiers. "Mon frère est très grand" = "My brother is very tall".

subject: french
prompt: In French, which verb do you use to talk about age?
explainer: In French you HAVE your age. "J'ai treize ans" literally means "I have thirteen years".

subject: french
explainer: Elle = she. Il = he. Je = I. Tu = you (informal).

subject: french
options: Je ne porte pas des lunettes.
Je ne porte pas de lunettes.
Je ne porte des lunettes pas.
explainer: In the negative, "des" or "une" change to "de" before the noun. So "Je ne porte pas DE lunettes".
audio: Je ne porte pas de lunettes

subject: french
explainer: Ma mère = my mother. Mon père = my father.

subject: french
prompt: "Dans ma famille, il y a quatre personnes." What does this say?
options: In my family there are four people
My family is in four places
explainer: "Il y a" = there is / there are. So: "In my family, there are four people."
audio: Dans ma famille, il y a quatre personnes

subject: french
explainer: Mon cousin = my (male) cousin. Female cousin = ma cousine.

subject: french
explainer: Un chien = a dog. Un chat = a cat. Useful for the pets topic.

subject: french
explainer: Habiter = to live (in a place). "J'habite à…" + city. "Je suis de…" = I'm from…

subject: french
explainer: La campagne = the countryside. Other useful: la ville (the city), la montagne (the mountain), la côte (the coast).

subject: french
prompt: Which is a French-speaking country in West Africa?
explainer: Sénégal is one of many French-speaking African countries (also Côte d'Ivoire, Mali, Cameroun).

subject: french
explainer: Petite = small (feminine, agreeing with maison). Big = grande. New = nouvelle. Old = vieille.

subject: french
explainer: Sympa (short for sympathique) = nice or friendly. Same form for masculine and feminine.

subject: french
explainer: Méchant(e) = mean, nasty, naughty. Opposite of sympa or gentil.

subject: french
prompt: Which sentence means "I like my brother because he is funny"?
options: Je n'aime pas mon frère parce qu'il est marrant
J'aime mon frère parce qu'il est marrant
Mon frère aime parce qu'il est marrant
explainer: J'aime = I like. Parce que = because. Marrant = funny. Useful pattern: "opinion + parce que + reason".
audio: J'aime mon frère parce qu'il est marrant

subject: french
explainer: Drôle = funny. Same form for masculine and feminine. Marrant(e) is another common word for funny.

explainer: Sœur = sister. The "oe" ligature is fine; many keyboards type it as two letters.

explainer: "Il y a" = there is / there are. Useful for describing your family size.

explainer: J'aime = I like / I love. "Les chiens" = dogs (plural).

explainer: Watch the linking sound: "j'ai-douz'-ans" — the 's' of douze flows into 'ans'.

explainer: The 'h' of habite is silent, so it sounds like "jabite". Londres = London.

subject: french
explainer: Frère has a soft 'è' — like the 'e' in 'bed'.

subject: french
prompt: Say it: "My birthday is in May".
explainer: Anniversaire links smoothly: "mon-naniversaire". Mai = May.

subject: french
explainer: The 'é' at the end is sharp — like the 'ay' in "day" but shorter.

subject: french
explainer: "Chiens" rhymes with the English "yen" — soft, nasal.

subject: french
explainer: Yeux = eyes. Bleus is plural to match yeux. Final 's' is silent.

subject: french
explainer: Pronunciation: "zhuh ma-PELL lay-OH". The 'p' in appelle is doubled in spelling but you only say one 'p'. Léo's accent on the 'é' makes the first vowel a clear 'ay' sound, not 'ee'.

subject: french
explainer: Said quickly: "komawn-tu-tapell". The "t'" is a contraction of "te".

subject: french
prompt: Say it: "Pleased to meet you" (boy speaking).
explainer: "an-shan-tay". Girls say "enchantée" — same sound, different spelling.

audio: Mon anniversaire est le quinze juin
prompt: Say it: "My birthday is on the 15th of June".
answer: Mon anniversaire est le quinze juin
explainer: "quinze" rhymes with "sanz" — not "kweenz". Anniversaire links smoothly: "mon-naniversaire".

explainer: "kel-ahzh-a-too". The "-tu" links to "as" so it sounds like one word.

subject: french
prompt: Say it: "It is November" (literally 'we are in November').
explainer: Two nasal vowels: "en" and "-vembre". Final 'e' of novembre is barely sounded.

subject: french
explainer: Cheveux is always plural. The 's' of "courts" stays silent.

subject: french
explainer: "yeux" rhymes with the French "feux". Final 's' on verts is silent.

subject: french
explainer: Porter = to wear (also for clothes). Lunettes is always plural in French.

subject: french
prompt: Say it: "I live in a house".
explainer: Silent 'h' on habite — sounds like "j'abite". Maison links smoothly with "une".

subject: french
explainer: Final 's' silent. "ang-lay" with a nasal vowel. Girl: "anglaise".

subject: french
prompt: Say it: "I live in the centre".
explainer: "san-tre" — the 'c' is soft before 'e'.

subject: french
audio: Dans ma famille il y a quatre personnes
prompt: Say it: "In my family there are four people".
answer: Dans ma famille il y a quatre personnes
explainer: "il y a" runs together: "ee-lee-ya". Quatre rhymes with "katra" — final 'e' barely there.

subject: french
audio: Je m'entends bien avec ma sœur
prompt: Say it: "I get on well with my sister".
answer: Je m'entends bien avec ma sœur
alternates: je mentends bien avec ma sœur
je mentends bien avec ma soeur
je m'entends bien avec ma soeur
explainer: "je m'entends" sounds like "juh-mon-ton". Sœur is one syllable: "sur".

subject: french
explainer: Final 't' on chat is silent: "sha". Un is nasal — like "uh~".

subject: french
explainer: "sym-pa" — short for sympathique. Same form for boys and girls.

subject: french
explainer: Feminine of marrant — the final 'te' is sounded. Boy: "marrant" (silent ending).

subject: french
explainer: Both consonants of "strict" sound: "streekt". Père has a soft 'è'.

subject: french
explainer: Final 'd' on grand is silent. Très is sharp: "treh".

subject: french
explainer: Feminine: "petite" — final 'te' sounds. Boy form: "petit" — silent ending.

subject: french
explainer: Final 't' silent: "in-tel-i-zhon". Girl form: intelligente — final 'te' sounds.

subject: french
audio: J'ai un frère et une sœur
prompt: Say it: "I have a brother and a sister".
answer: J'ai un frère et une sœur
alternates: jai un frère et une soeur
j'ai un frère et une soeur
jai un frere et une soeur
explainer: "et" = and — pronounced "ay". Final 'e' of frère barely there.

subject: french
explainer: Use "je suis de" + city to say where you're from. "Leicester" stays in English form.

subject: french
explainer: Lille = a city in northern France. Double 'l' makes a soft glide; pronounce "leel".

subject: french
prompt: Translate: 'I do NOT have a brother.'
explainer: Negation in French: ne … pas. After 'pas' in the negative, 'un/une/des' usually become 'de'. So 'pas un frère' becomes 'pas de frère'.

subject: french
prompt: Which phrase means 'I never eat fish'?
options: Je ne mange jamais de poisson
explainer: Ne … jamais = never. 'Jamais' replaces 'pas' to mean 'never' instead of just 'not'.

subject: french
explainer: Wrap the verb in ne … pas: elle EST heureuse becomes elle N'EST PAS heureuse. 'Ne' becomes 'n'' before a vowel.

subject: french
explainer: Rien = nothing. Used with 'ne': 'Je ne mange rien' = 'I am eating nothing.'

subject: french
explainer: J'aime = I like / I love. 'Aimer' is one of the first verbs you learn — often paired with school subjects, hobbies, food.
